metadata.rubric: Manual 1-5 scoring per dimension: coherence, factuality, relevance, repetition (5=no repetition), completeness
metadata.prompt: Explain the concept of virtual memory in operating systems, including how paging works and why it matters for large applications.
metadata.note: All three models use same base (Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct); only quantization level differs.

baseline_warmup.output_text: Virtual memory is a technique used by operating systems to allow programs to access larger amounts of memory than are physically available... Paging is one
baseline_warmup.notes: Clear, accurate definition; correctly introduces paging; truncated at 64-token limit but logically consistent.

quant_q8_0.output_text: Virtual memory is a technique used in operating systems to allow a computer to have more than one address space, or memory space, for different programs... The operating system then uses a
quant_q8_0.notes: Confuses virtual memory with address-space isolation; misses paging explanation; "memory space" repeated 3x; factually imprecise but not wrong.

quant_q4_k_m.output_text: Virtual memory is a technique used in operating systems to increase the amount of memory available to a computer. It allows a computer to allocate more memory than is physically available, without having to physically move data between physical memory and disk...
quant_q4_k_m.notes: Clear and accurate; slight imprecision (VM does involve disk for swap, not avoid it); no paging reached at 64 tokens; fluent and on-topic.

finding: FP16 produces the highest quality output (23/25). Q4_K_M is nearly equivalent (22/25). Q8_0 is noticeably lower (17/25) due to imprecise factual framing, but still coherent. Counter-intuitively, Q4_K_M slightly outperforms Q8_0 at this model scale — likely due to different GGUF quantization kernel implementations in llama.cpp.
